title: Constructing arguments of system commands from user input is security-sensitive
description: Constructing arguments of system commands from user input is security-sensitiveAnalyze your codeintentionality-logicalsecuritySecurity HotspotcweinjectionConstructing arguments of system commands from user input is security-sensitive. It has led in the past to the following vulnerabilities:CVE-2016-9920CVE-2021-29472Arguments of system commands are processed by the executed program. The arguments are usually used to configure and influence the behavior of the programs.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

logic.detection_conditions: Detects Process.Start calls or ProcessStartInfo.Arguments assignments that use string concatenation with variables, suggesting user input being passed to system commands
logic.triggering_conditions: Triggers when ProcessStartInfo.Arguments or Process.Start includes concatenated strings with command-like patterns (cmd.exe, powershell.exe, etc.) AND variable concatenation is present, excluding validated input and const values
logic.exclusion_conditions: Excludes Path.GetFileName usage (validated input), const/readonly values, logging statements, test code, and scenarios with input validation functions
logic.reasoning: Command injection occurs when user input is directly concatenated into system command arguments without validation. The rule detects assignments to ProcessStartInfo.Arguments or Process.Start calls that include string concatenation with variables. Common attack vectors include cmd.exe, powershell.exe, and bash commands. Path.GetFileName is excluded as it strips directory traversal. Input validation methods are excluded as they indicate sanitization. Const values cannot contain injected input.

should_not_trigger: const string args = "/c dir"; Process.Start("cmd.exe", args);
[TestMethod] public void Test() { proc.StartInfo.Arguments = "/c echo test"; }
